If the market crashed, we would unwind the position.

If liquidity falls next week, we will unwind the position.
Sergio, thank you for the call. Let me start with a hypothetical. If our bank froze your credit line tomorrow, what would your desk do?
That is a fair question. If you froze the line, we would post more collateral the same day and protect the desk.
And if the market crashed at the same time? Would you still cover the exposure?
If the market crashed, we would not panic. We would negotiate a waiver and offer a small concession, not a fire sale.
Good. So, a real proposal now: if we made a counteroffer with a lower credit line, would your committee accept it?
If the downside were limited, we would accept it. If it threatened the buffer, we would ask for more time.
If a partner bank became insolvent, we would cut the exposure at once.
If you froze our credit line, we would post more collateral.
If the downside were limited, we would accept the counteroffer.
If I were you, I would make a counteroffer, not accept the first one.
We would negotiate a waiver instead of a fire sale.

Hello, Sergio. This is the supervisory team at the central bank, with one hypothetical for your review. Imagine a severe but unlikely scenario. If a major partner bank became insolvent overnight, what would your desk do? If that bank froze its credit line, would you have enough collateral to cover the exposure? We are not saying this will happen. We only want to know how you would react if it did. If you needed a waiver on a limit, you would ask us first, not after. Please send us your answer in writing before the review. If your plan were clear, the whole process would be much faster. Thank you.

Hi Sergio, it is Paulo from the credit desk. A quick thought before the negotiation with the partner bank. I have run the hypothetical. If they cut our credit line by half, we would still be fine, but the buffer would be tight. If they asked for more collateral, I would offer a small concession, not a big one. Honestly, if I were you, I would not accept their first offer. I would make a counteroffer with a longer deadline. If the downside stayed limited, we would sign; if it grew, we would walk away. Call me before the meeting. Thanks.

First, imagine the partner bank made a surprise move. If the bank froze our credit line, we would post more collateral the same day. If they asked for a concession, we would offer a small one, not a big one. If the market crashed at the same time, we would negotiate a waiver instead of a fire sale. Finally, if their counteroffer protected the buffer, Sergio would accept it; if it did not, he would ask for more time.
